stem: Consider the system \( kx + ([[b]])y = [[r1]] \) and \( ([[c]])x + [[d]]y = [[r2]] \), where \( k \) is a real parameter. For which value of \( k \) does the system fail to have a unique solution?
solution: The system has a unique solution exactly when its coefficient matrix \( \begin{bmatrix} k & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & [[d]] \end{bmatrix} \) is invertible, i.e. when \( [[d]]k - ([[b]])([[c]]) \ne 0 \). It fails to be unique when \( [[d]]k = ([[b]])([[c]]) \), so \( k = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the product \( bc \); one must still divide by \( [[d]] \) to solve \( [[d]]k = bc \).
This solves the determinant equation with the wrong sign.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: In the system below, the second equation's constant term and \(x\)-coefficient are already [[m]] times the first equation's: \( [[a]]x + ([[b]])y = [[r1]] \) and \( [[ma]]x + ky = [[r2]] \), where \(k\) is a real parameter. For which value of \(k\) does the system have infinitely many solutions?
solution: The second equation is exactly \([[m]]\) times the first precisely when its \(y\)-coefficient matches that same ratio: \(k = [[m]]\cdot([[b]]) = [[answer]]\). At \(k=[[answer]]\) the second equation is literally [[m]] times the first, so every point on that line solves both equations: infinitely many solutions.
distractor_rationales: This is the first equation's \(y\)-coefficient itself, without scaling by [[m]].
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is the (already-scaled) \(x\)-coefficient of the second equation, not the value needed for the \(y\)-coefficient.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: In the system below, the second equation's \(x\)-coefficient is already [[m]] times the first equation's, but its constant term is not: \( [[a]]x + ([[b]])y = [[r1]] \) and \( [[ma]]x + ky = [[r2]] \), where \(k\) is a real parameter. For which value of \(k\) does the system have NO solution?
solution: At \(k=[[m]]\cdot([[b]]) = [[answer]]\), the coefficient rows become proportional (the second equation's left-hand side is [[m]] times the first's), but since \([[r2]] \ne [[m]]\cdot([[r1]])\) the constant terms are NOT in that same ratio. The two equations describe parallel, non-coincident lines, so the system has no solution at \(k = [[answer]]\).
distractor_rationales: This is the first equation's \(y\)-coefficient itself, without scaling by [[m]].
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is the (already-scaled) \(x\)-coefficient of the second equation, not the \(y\)-coefficient value that makes the rows proportional.
This is the first equation's constant term, not the value of \(k\).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Consider the system \( [[a]]x + ([[b]])y = [[r1]] \) and \( [[ma]]x + ([[mb]])y = k \), where \(k\) is a real parameter (the second equation's left-hand side is exactly [[m]] times the first's). For which value of \(k\) does the system have infinitely many solutions?
solution: Since the left-hand side of the second equation already equals [[m]] times the first's, the two equations describe the same line exactly when the right-hand sides are in that same ratio: \(k = [[m]]\cdot([[r1]]) = [[answer]]\). At that value the second equation is literally [[m]] times the first, giving infinitely many solutions.
distractor_rationales: This is the first equation's constant term itself, without scaling by [[m]].
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is the (already-scaled) \(x\)-coefficient of the second equation, not the required right-hand side.
This is the (already-scaled) \(y\)-coefficient of the second equation, not the required right-hand side.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
